data.title: GE Aerospace: One Of My Highest Conviction Long-Term Compounders
data.description:
Introduction

Technically speaking, if you own a company for at least ten years, you’ll witness no less than 40 quarterly earnings calls (in some countries, that number will be less). All of these have the ability to add serious volatility to your investment. And as someone who has a concentrated portfolio of less than 20 stocks, I feel this at least four times a year.

That said, I believe many take earnings season too seriously. While it’s important to assess the health of America’s biggest companies and the investments in your own portfolio, we shouldn’t forget that it’s just one moment in time. Besides that, its earnings are impossible to predict, and the market’s reaction to earnings will be. I am sure that everyone who has been investing for more than a year knows exactly what I’m talking about.

  • Sometimes a company sells off hard after reporting higher-than-expected earnings.
  • Sometimes a company rallies hard after reporting the worst earnings in years.

It’s tricky because there are so many things to keep in mind, including actual earnings versus analyst guidance, the company’s own forward-looking guidance, and the general market environment. After all, earnings are backward-looking. If a company tells you it generated $2 in income last quarter, it doesn’t tell us anything about the future.

Hence, as much as I like earnings season (I’m a big fan despite everything I just wrote), I don’t really care for all the small numbers. Whether a company beat estimates by 1 penny or had some temporary weakness in some segment due to an unforeseen factor, I don’t really care about that.

I care about the Big Picture and how a company positions itself to create long-term value.

Today, we’re talking about GE Aerospace (GE), which has become one of my largest holdings and one of my favorite industrial compounders, as it has a market share of roughly 75% in commercial engines, is a leading military engine producer, and enjoys the tailwinds from secular commercial growth and what has become a booming defense industry.

However, right now, there are at least two major issues:

  • The war in Iran has substantially hurt commercial aerospace due to safety risks in massive hubs like Dubai and inflationary concerns that reduce ticket affordability.
  • GE Aerospace isn’t cheap - and that’s putting it mildly.

My most recent article was written on October 22.

As the company behind the GE ticker just released its earnings, we have a lot of material to work through.

So, let’s get to it!

This Beast Is Firing On All Cylinders

For starters, GE did exactly what I have gotten used to, which is reporting elevated growth across the board. If we take a look at the handy overview below, we see that orders increased by almost 90%, while adjusted revenue rose by 29%. Operating profit rose by 18%. Adjusted EPS rose by 25%. Free cash flow rose by 14%.

And, under the hood, we see that order growth was strong in all segments, as the CES segment (that’s the commercial engine and services segment) saw 93% higher revenues. DPT (that’s defense operations) orders were up 67%. To add more context, in defense, the book-to-bill ratio was above 2, which means for every dollar in finished work, it got two dollars worth of new orders, which is indicative of elevated future growth.

It now has more than $210 billion in backlog after winning more than 650 new commercial engine deals in the first quarter alone. This includes:

  • More than 300 LEAP-1A engines for American Airlines to power its new A321XLR fleet.
  • A massive order for the United Airlines 787 fleet, which makes United the biggest operator of GEnx engines.
  • Delta Airlines also selected 60 GEnx engines for its 787 fleet.

That’s what I love about GE, as I don’t have to select winners in the airline space or bet on a specific plane builder like Boeing (BA). As long as I’m invested in GE and it is able to maintain on top of its game, I automatically win in an aerospace bull market.

Unfortunately, there are some problems.

Guidance Is Strong, Yet Cracks Are Visible

I have been very bullish on commercial aerospace for many years. I even minored in Aerospace Management and Operations, which is something I will never forget, as I won the airline management game and accidentally enrolled in a math course that nearly caused me to fail.

Even back then, we were told that investing in airlines is a great way to lose money. Every expert we talked to liked suppliers, which is another reason why I majored in Purchasing & Supply Management. We also had a talk from an expert who discussed the history of commercial aerospace. This included major deals like the “Open Skies” agreement that allowed airlines from the Middle East to use EU airspace and EU carriers to enter Middle Eastern airspace.

Back then, Western companies thought they had made a great deal, as nobody could have expected just how big a factor nations like the United Arab Emirates would become. Now, they are the big hotspot that connects major continents.

I’m telling you that because the war in Iran has caused these hotspots to weaken. On top of the inflation hitting consumers and airlines, this has created security risks that temporarily seem to hurt a market that I expect has decades of high secular growth potential.

Although GE Aerospace shouldn’t get a big hit from the war due to the lagging impact on engine maintenance demand, the fact that it revised global traffic lower shows that the commercial growth thesis has some cracks, at least for the time being:

In the first quarter, global departures were up low single digits, including a high single-digit decline in the Middle East which represents roughly 5% of our departures. And for the balance of the year, we've assessed multiple scenarios to develop a range of outcomes with our current assumption that the conflict and its effects continue through the summer.

As a result, we're reducing our full year departures outlook from mid-single-digit growth to flat, to low single-digit growth. This includes a low double-digit decline in the Middle East for the year. with modest reductions to other regions. Based on our experience during the global financial crisis, the impact of services will likely lag changes in air traffic demand by several quarters. to be followed by a period of above-average growth. - GE 1Q26 Earnings Call.

And while GE doesn’t forecast a global recession, it was very clear about the negative impact of high fuel prices on flight demand. Luckily, it benefits from defense, which offsets some commercial headwinds due to strong demand in almost every program, including the Black Hawk, the Apache, the B1, the B2, the F-15, the F-16, and the Eurofighter.

These are just some programs that GE powers.

Nonetheless, despite beating Q1 '26 EPS of $1.86 by $0.26, GE didn’t hike its guidance. As a rule of thumb, whenever a company doesn’t hike guidance after a big beat, something is off.

Right now, the problem is obviously uncertainty with regard to the war and inflation. The good news is that the company is still trending towards the high end of its range of low double-digit revenue growth and EPS of $7.20 to $7.40. It expects up to $8.4 billion in free cash flow, which would translate to a free cash flow conversion rate of more than 100%.

In other words, more than 100% of earnings are expected to be turned into free cash flow, which is a sign of high-quality earnings.

This is partially due to FLIGHT DECK, which is the company’s program to enhance operations and overall efficiencies. It now sees roughly $4 billion in service revenue growth this year, roughly $500 million more than initially expected.

While operating margins fell by 200 basis points to 21.8%, that’s due to the impact of installed engine growth, investments, and inflation. Essentially, on top of some economic headwinds (inflation), the company boosted its installed base, which paves the road for higher margins down the road, as most engines are sold close to cost. Most money is made on maintenance. That explains why the phase where total unit demand is high (right now) isn’t the period with the highest margins.

Looking ahead, thanks to high demand, FLIGHT DECK, and accelerating maintenance demand, I think this company has a long runway of high growth ahead. But more on that in the next part.

Valuation

While I am writing this, GE is up more than 60% over the past 12 months.

So, generally speaking, expectations are high. Its valuation tells the same story, as GE shares trade at a blended P/E ratio in the low-40 range. That’s not cheap, at least not in the “traditional” sense. It explains why the past three earnings all came with a selloff. Today isn’t different, as GE shares are down roughly 5% while I am writing this.

Immediately after the release, they were up 4% pre-market.

What changed?

The market figured out that selling the good news may be a good move.

And, as bullish as I may be, I’m fine with that. Let them (bearish investors) sell.

Right now, we’re dealing with a company that is expected to grow its EPS by 16% this year, potentially followed by 16% growth in 2027 and 13% growth in 2028. If my thesis is right, these numbers will last way beyond 2028, as GE is just getting started scaling its commercial business and maintenance division. This will work wonders for margins.

Even better, the growth path is so favorable that even if GE were to get re-rated to 30x earnings, investors wouldn’t lose money over the next 2-3 years (theoretically speaking).

Although I currently lack the cash to buy more, which will change once I am settled in Albania after May 1 and have transferred my cash and stock, I am closely watching this stock. The closer I can get it to $270, the better. Despite its valuation, I think we’re in buy territory, which may explain why the average price target is $350, more than 20% above its current price, with an average Wall Street rating of Strong Buy, according to Seeking Alpha.

That’s why I stick to a Buy rating myself and believe it’s one of the best long-term compounders investors can buy on weakness - across all sectors.

Also, because of all of this, GE is a TOLL stock, as it has tangible assets, limited competition, the ability to scale margins due to limited new CapEx demand, and long-lasting revenue flows due to maintenance contracts. In general, as we have been working a lot more on the TOLL theses at Main Street Alpha, you’ll hear me talk about this way more.

Takeaway

I invest in compounding machines. That includes GE.

As much fun as we had last year, this year is more challenging. That’s not because of internal struggles, but simply because the valuation has become a bit stretched. That, too, isn’t an issue. However, as long as the Iran War threatens commercial aerospace growth, investors would rather sell the good news.

For me, that opens up new opportunities to buy the dip, which I will soon gladly do.

The biggest risks are a prolonged weakness in global air traffic, runaway inflation that airlines cannot offset, and or mismanagement in its large maintenance programs.

data.title: Putting In A Stock Market Top
data.description:
The first part of the article is a bit data-detail dense about how the rate of return has varied for five fixed length trading-day-periods. This gives context to recent market moves with implications for what is coming in the short term. The latter part of the article looks from a much longer term context and considers the possibility of a once in a lifetime market move.

Wildly Over Sold to Wildly Overbought

On March 30th the S&P 500 closed at 6344, then rose to 7126 on April 17th. This corresponds with the percentile rank of short term period of return rising from 3.6 to 98.1 which appears as a spike in the green line of of Figure# 1. What this means is that the short term period of return went from performing worse than 96.4% of such returns to out performing all but 1.9% of such return periods.

Figure #1

The green line is based on the price change in 12.5-day-trading periods (Figure #2) and 25-day-trading periods (Figure #3)

Figure #2

The 10.4% price increase in the 12.5-trading-day period ending Friday the 17th was higher than 99.7% of such periods in the last 20 years. Monday’s decline brought the rank down to 99.3. On March 30, the low point before the spike, the percentile rank had only been 4.9.

The best fit sine wave (rose colored line) to this trading-day period estimated the strongest period in this cycle would complete on April 21, it probably completed on the 17th. The black date labels on the chart mark estimated peaks while the red date labels are for estimated troughs.

Figure #3

25-trading-day change had a 91.6 percentile rank on April 17 up from 4.5 at the end of March. With Monday’s decline the magenta marker shows the rank at 91.4. The best fit sine wave suggests this cycle will reach its peak May 1.

Figure #4

While the 50-trading-day change is not used to calculate the short-term percentile rank in Figure #1 it was down to a 7.3 rank on March 30. It rose to almost average with a rank of 48.5 shown in Figure #5 for Monday. The fitted wave suggests a peak 50-trading-day period for April 28.

Figure #5

Cycles for the three shorted trading-day periods were all pointing up for the three-week period of the big surge to new market highs. Soon they will all be pointing down

Pointing Down

Cycles for the two longer periods 100 and 200 trading-day periods have been pointing down for some time. In the next few days, the three shorter cycles will join in the downward estimate. All five cycles pointing down at the same time is a rarity.

Figure #6

The 100-trading-day price change as of Monday the 20th at 5.2% is just above average with a percentile rank of 51.4. While the fitted sine wave for the 100-trading-day period shown in Figure #6 tends to have the poorest correlation of the five fitted sine waves, I believe it still adds to understanding where the market is and may be going. The sine wave suggests a week period ending June 12, 2026.

Figure #6

The fit for the 200-day-trading period (Figure #6) usually has the best or second-best (out of five) correlation with the actual data. It currently suggests the worst 200-trading day period for this cycle will complete on August 26, 2026.

The adage. “Sell in May and go away.” is probably bad advice most years, however the five cycles and the percentile ranks from figure #1 suggest it could be good advice in 2026.

Decline, But What Kind?

The steep increase in the last three weeks that pushed to a 99.7 percentile rank for the shortest of the five periods makes at least some kind of decline or flat lining almost certain. While I expect at least a correction to start sometime before May 1, the data comprising the trading-day period returns and best fit cycles obviously is not adequate to guarantee a market top in that time frame. Sentiment to unknown future events could certainly overwhelm the data presented.

While a recession has been much longer coming than I expected a couple of years ago data suggests we still are headed for an economic recession.

Figure #7

Year over year job growth is now down to 0.2%. Figure #7 shows this is weaker than it has been in the first month of the last 8 recessions.

Figure #8

When the rate of economic growth is lower than the rate of inflation the economy almost always falls into a recession before robust growth resumes. Figure #8 shows the difference of the Y/Y GDP growth rate minus the Y/Y rate of inflation in the GDP implicit price deflator. When the line is above zero growth is higher than inflation. The line below zero shows inflation running hotter than growth.

We have had five straight quarters of faster inflation. The last twelve recessions all correspond with inflation running hotter than growth usually before the recession starts.

In the last 80 years the only time inflation ran hotter without a recession occurring was with the wave of inflation in 1921 and 2022 following the Covid pandemic. This did bring a bear market in stocks, but the ubiquitous forecasts of recession proved false.

Usually, a recession starts within a year of inflation running hotter. The big exception to this was ahead of the 1980 recession where inflation ran hotter for four straight years.

So far the combination of inflation running hotter than growth and the year over year rate of job growth dropping below 0.4% has not made any false calls of a recession coming.

Valuation

Stock market valuation suggests potential declines could be massive. In January the price to sales ratio for the S&P 500 hit 3.39 (Figure #9).

Figure #9

Updates of price to sales at dkydj.

A dollar of annual sales for the S&P 500 was priced 59% higher in January 2026 than it was at the peak valuation leading into the dot com bust from 2000 into 2003. It took seven years for the S&P 500 to pass the 2000 high. If you priced the market with inflation adjusted dollars the new high took 14 years.

One variable that at least in the short run explains or rationalizes the extraordinary high price to sales ratio is high profit margins. Figure #10 looks at profit from the economy wide perspective rather than just the S&P 500.

Figure #10

Corporate profit almost hit 14% of GDP in the 4th quarter of 2025 setting a new record. While people benefit from high wages and low prices, profit benefits from low wages and high prices.

Figure #10 also shows pay to workers dropping near the lowest ever share of GDP. This low share is kind of remarkable given we are near the peak of an economic expansion. Recessions make workers more vulnerable and easier for capitalists to squeeze or hold down wages and salaries. The all-time low share for wages followed the great recession and financial crisis.

Record profit margins feel wonderful to capitalists and are keeping a narrow healthy-looking spread between the yield of high rated corporate debt and junk bonds. However, record profit margins also mean the biggest squeeze on workers and consumers since at least WWII.

During the previous six months GDP probably grew at about a 1% pace or less. The real economy appears to be following the squeeze on workers and consumers. At some point, perhaps between now and August, the weakness of the real economy could show up in corporate earnings and stock prices.

Recent stock gains are almost certainly headed for some kind of consolidation. They could be headed for a typical recessionary bear market. It might be prudent to consider there is at least some small chance that the 40-year squeeze of workers share of the pie dropping from 50% to 42% could bring a collapse in demand for goods and services that collapses market valuation back to what was normal 50 years ago. From 1947—1995 the price to sales ratio for the S&P 500 averaged 0.77 about 77% lower than it is now. 12-month-trailing PE averaged 14.2 between 1947 and 1996 vs a 26.1 average the last 20 years.

An important point about valuation is that it has little or no influence over the direction of stock prices in the next month or year. No matter how overvalued a market is sentiment can always drive it higher in the short term.

Valuation tends to have strong correlation with stock market return over periods of ten to fifteen years. So the high valuation now could mean stock market return over the next fifteen years does not keep up with inflation.

Measures of valuation heading ever higher may be the new normal. It could also be a cycle that reverses. In the last 25 years real GDP has grown at a 2.1% pace, nominal GDP has grown at a 4.5% rate. If profit margins stop expanding and economic growth continues at the rate of the last 25 years profits will average growing 4.5%. If profit margins trend down to what use to be normal earnings growth could be much weaker or even negative. The market is priced for double digit earnings growth. The possibility of disappointment is real.

data.title: Software Is 2026's Strongest Buy Opportunity: Here's Why
data.description:
A Reading Error

Let’s begin with the facts. My belief is that the facts are the place to begin. The software segment suffered some very serious setbacks in 2026. I am referring here to major players in the industry, not speculative companies or small, unknown players. Major players such as Adobe (down by 29%), Salesforce (down by 30%), ServiceNow (down by 35%), Workday (down by 40%), and Atlassian (for many investors, a synonym for “good” software) down by as much as 56%! With regards to the overall loss of these companies relative to their peak values, things get even worse. However, I’ve named a number of players, however it appears that essentially every player in the segment experienced a massive price drop. The drop was indiscriminate and left no one unscathed.

Ultimately, there is a very straightforward answer as to why this happened. Investors became very fearful that artificial intelligence would render obsolete the long-standing competitive advantage held by most traditional software companies. Many investors were thinking as follows: if AI could generate code, automate tasks and develop new applications faster and better than humans could, then the advantages that previously existed for traditional software companies would soon evaporate. While this is a simple and somewhat intuitive concept, I feel it simplifies matters far too much. For one thing, it does not take into account the fact that software companies are not all the same. Their business models are very different (we'll talk about this later.)

I also strongly believe that the market has made a huge mistake and when markets make large mistakes, it leads to market opportunities that must be capitalized upon. I will begin with something that I’m confident everyone can agree with: AI itself does not create value – regardless of how complex or capable. An AI model is nothing more than an engine. Without the rest of the vehicle (the steering, braking system, dash, GPS and chassis) an engine cannot cause a vehicle to move. Similarly, in the enterprise space, the vehicle consists of workflow management software, databases, integration software for disparate systems, permissioning mechanisms, security measures and memory for the company’s operational functions. That is why I refer to this group of components as the orchestration layer. I’ll explain using an example: the orchestra is equivalent to the control center. It is the point at which decisions regarding where to utilize AI models, with what data, under what constraints, in what sequence and with what type of control occur. Whoever owns that control center (the decision-making authority) in my opinion, has increased in value since the beginning of this year more than the current stock prices reflect.

That is why I remain convinced that the prevailing narrative is incorrect. The best software is not being destroyed by AI. Rather, AI is creating significant problems for weak, shallow or easily replicated software. At the same time, AI is increasing the value of software involved directly in business operations. Those companies that operate at the level of true systems of records and/or provide a platform for running AI-based workloads do not appear to be experiencing any issues whatsoever. On the contrary, they seem to be gaining greater control over their businesses and rapidly monetizing this improved position more quickly than investors currently realize. Therefore, I believe this presents us with an investment opportunity at a time when stock prices have dropped as if the underlying business model had been severely damaged – while simultaneously, in my opinion, the competitive capabilities of many of these companies are becoming stronger rather than weaker.

Not all software is the same

In advance, I would like to make one very easy distinction. There are at least three different kinds of models (that coexist and compete) that exist today in AI related software.

  • First type; "Frontier LLMs," Large Generalized Models. The Frontier LLMs are able to produce text, code, images, and answers in short order and possess an obvious advantage when it comes to speed of innovation and product delivery to consumers.
  • Second type: "AI-Native Apps," new applications that were born around AI. The AI native apps are nimble, grow rapidly, have a more flexible price structure, and can serve many different targeted niches.
  • Third type; I believe the market has treated this too superficially. "SaaS Incumbents," The large enterprise software already installed inside of companies.

As you can see in the Goldman Sachs chart shown below, while the Frontier LLMs may be more innovative on the front end, the AI natives are certainly more aggressive on price, however, the SaaS incumbents are still far superior on two areas that really do matter in the real world: customer relationships and domain expertise. And this is more than a small detail.

Value is moving upward

Recently, I read a Morgan Stanley note. While I will admit that I was somewhat pleased by their agreement with my March thesis, better late than never. According to the note I read, Morgan Stanley argues that AI is shifting from simple generation to a three-tier architecture consisting of LLMs as the "brain", Orchestration as the "system", and Memory as "knowledge". Simply put, having the most intelligent model is no longer sufficient, you now require the additional layer that determines how to arrange the steps needed to accomplish a task, retrieves the proper data, determines the sequence of events that needs to occur to complete a job, and links the AI to external tooling and the companies' real-world processes. Morgan Stanley does a good job of putting it simply: orchestration is rapidly becoming the new bottleneck due to the fact that in agentic workloads, the value is rapidly increasing towards coordination, memory and workflow management and away from simple generation.

And it is this exact reason that I am continuing to believe that investors are significantly undervaluing the major Enterprise Software incumbents. The companies that act as Systems of Record (i.e., the company's true operational repository) begin with a competitive edge. These companies have existing history of use data, compliance rules, audit trails, permission levels, change control and integrations developed over years of usage- plus, they are already at the center of every important customer process. This totally alters the landscape. A company won't quickly switch out its CRM system, HR Platform or Workflow System it used for ten years because a less expensive AI agent came available. We aren't discussing replacing an iPhone App here. We are discussing modifying the infrastructure that a company uses to manage its customers, employees, documents, approvals, controls and internal obligations. In the real world, what matters is continuity, security, governance and operational memory.

I referenced this in my thesis back in March that discussed "AURA," or Assets that are worth a whole lot more than just code because they include Proprietary Data, Integration Architectures, Workflows and Relationships Built Over Time. Plus, so called data gravity makes it progressively more expensive and risky to move these business processes somewhere else.

Thus, in my view the actual war isn't between "Old Software" and "New AI." Its between those who own the work orchestration layer and those who provide an easily reproducible function. And once this battle is fought there, the large incumbents will be significantly closer to where the battle is taking place than current thinking in the market suggests.

Monetization

In my view, the most compelling aspect of this (and what will likely be the area where companies can point to success), is that there is now evidence (in the form of actual financial performance) that benefits of orchestrating AI/ML across enterprise systems are no longer simply theoretical concepts in research reports, they are being realized in terms of dollars.

For example, Workday (WDAY) is arguably one of the most obvious examples. During Q4 2025, Workday generated over $100M of new Annual Contract Value (“ACV”) from AI-based products, experienced greater than 100% year-over-year growth in ACV, and achieved total Annual Recurring Revenue (“ARR”) exceeding $400M for AI-based solutions. But it wasn’t just the amount of money that was exciting, it was how much of it came from existing customers. Approximately half of all deals completed within Workday’s existing customer base included at least one AI-based solution and deals that included an AI-based solution were on average approximately 50% larger than the overall deal size for Workday’s portfolio. Additionally, early adopters of the Self-Service Agent reported a 25% reduction in HR-related tickets and a 20% increase in employee productivity.

It appears that the same trend is unfolding outside of Workday as well. For instance, Adobe (ADBE) has reportedly achieved $250M in revenue from “AI-first” products a quarter ahead of schedule and doubled those revenues in six months, representing approximately 13% of the net new annual recurring revenue for Digital Media for the last six months. Additionally, Firefly’s generative credit consumption has increased more than 45% quarter-over-quarter and the company has indicated that ARR influenced by AI has exceeded $5B.

Salesforce (CRM) has stated that its Data Cloud & AI ARR exceeded $1.2B during Q2 2026 and that agentic AI ARR represented $440M, which is up 400% year-over-year. Further, Salesforce noted that it has closed more than 12,500 Agentforce deals since launching the program.

Similarly, ServiceNow (NOW) anticipates reaching $500M in Annual Contract Value (“ACV”) for Now Assist by the end of the calendar year and has set a goal of reaching $1B in ACV for the product by the next calendar year.

As for Snowflake (SNOW), it has announced that it has reached an AI run rate of $100M a quarter ahead of expectations and that its product revenue grew 30% year-over-year. Moreover, Snowflake stated that the contribution of AI workloads to its growth has been increasing.

And finally, Microsoft continues to demonstrate strong numbers as well. According to numerous estimates, roughly 20 percentage points of Azure’s growth currently derives from generative AI, a number that could potentially exceed those of most other large software peers.

Who Wins and Who Loses

At this point, in my opinion, the differentiation clearly emerges. All software does not go in the same direction; thus, the market has made an error in selling nearly all software without having sufficient discrimination. To date, the primary winners of AI are in the software infrastructure layers: cybersecurity, databases, DevOps, data platforms, and content delivery networks.

Why? It is easy to explain. As AI begins to penetrate into business processes, three things grow:

  • Computing power requirements,
  • Data complexity, and
  • Security needs.

As each factor grows, then the value of the software that runs the overall machine grows. Not coincidentally, the underlying software layer that includes infrastructure, data/AI platforms and cybersecurity has shown relative resiliency based on the fact that it will benefit directly from this new level of technology. In fact, I believe that cyber security is one area of the greatest protection: AI does not diminish the need for security, it creates additional security needs due to emerging threats such as prompt injection attacks and the expanded attack surface of enterprise systems. Similarly, companies that hold a system of record status and have unique or proprietary data (e.g., ERP, Payroll, PLM, EDA, Construction Software) and other well-integrated verticals are ones that, in my opinion, are starting out with an advantage as they protect a company's true data and have typically had involvement in critical business processes for many years.

Conversely, there are obviously some losers that are somewhat obvious. At least partially correct about this is the market. Areas that are most exposed include tasks that are more repetitive, conversational, standardized and have lower operational risk. Contact Centers and Marketing are likely among the most extreme examples of this category of software and its potential exposure to agentic automation and may represent a full adoption (i.e. 100%) of functionality. Close to contact centers and marketing are Sales Force and Commerce.

All front-office marketing, sales, and customer-service applications are significantly exposed to multiple compression. The market correctly identifies the large risk that a significant amount of workload can be assumed by AI Agents.

Thus, I believe that the largest opportunity exists in this area, not in simply purchasing "software", but in recognizing that the market has grouped together very disparate businesses. The market has penalized both truly vulnerable platforms and companies whose role within their respective industries is becoming stronger through AI. Thus, I continue to see opportunities in infrastructure, security, vertical software and orchestration platforms.

Valuations

When I look at the area of valuations, I feel like this is where the disconnect really shows up. When you take into account how the valuation of each company compares to their respective business quality, and compare them against future earnings revisions, it seems to be that investors are continuing to treat so many companies as if they're broke (with the exception of some very poor companies), even though their underlying numbers tell a completely different story.

Adobe’s forward P/E multiple is approximately 10.3x a level the market hasn't recognized in over five years. Since then, Adobe's AI first products have generated $250M+ in revenue, with the product doubling in just six months. Workday is trading on a forward P/E multiple of 11.73x, and has a PEG ratio of 0.6. To me, that represents one of the most attractive multiples in the high-quality growth software space. Workday has already surpassed $400M in AI ARR, and has grown by over 100%.

ServiceNow, as one of the leading players in Enterprise Orchestration continues to trade at a 23.8x forward P/E multiple with a PEG of 0.95. It also sports a 90% buy rating from analysts, as you can see below. Despite all of these positives, the stock has still declined roughly 35% YTD while estimate revisions have increased by 2.5% in the last three months.

In addition, the SaaS sector as a whole has experienced a dramatic decline in value. As a result, the group now sits at a 28x P/E multiple, representing the lowest level we've seen in ten years. With respect to the risk/reward ratio for quality businesses, many investment firms believe the current levels are becoming increasingly attractive.

Company EV/Sales (LTM) Forward P/E Revenue Est. ($M) LT Growth (%)
Microsoft 10.3x 23.9x 81,423 17.8%
Salesforce 3.9x 14.1x 11,054 –
ServiceNow 7.2x 23.8x 3,745 –
Adobe 4.1x 10.3x 6,459 –
Workday 3.3x 11.73x 2,516 –
Snowflake 10.8x 83.0x 1,325 23.7%
Palantir 76.4x 111.6x 1,537 47.6%
Datadog 12.5x 59.3x – 24.4%

The most contrarian factor is reflected within the revision data. Specifically, Palantir has seen EPS upgrades of approximately 30% in three months and the stock is still down approximately 18% YTD. While Snowflake saw positive revisions of around 10% and the stock has fallen approximately 31%. Additionally, HubSpot was upgraded by approximately 8% and the stock dropped approximately 43%. Historically speaking, divergent conditions such as improving fundamentals and declining prices create the perfect opportunity for top entry points in quality growth sectors (source: Bloomberg Terminal).

Company 3-Month EPS Revision (%) Consensus EPS Signal
Palantir +30.1% $1.31 Explosive AI traction
Snowflake +10.2% $1.81 AI workload inflection
HubSpot +8.1% $12.43 Vertical resilience
Microsoft +5.5% $16.87 Azure AI contribution
ServiceNow +2.5% $4.20 Orchestration scaling
Salesforce +0.07% $13.19 Stable execution
Adobe 0.0% $23.52 Firefly ramp beginning
Workday -2.5% $10.49 Near-term caution

Risks

The bear case should not be taken lightly because it has real merit. There are many risks facing the software sector, and some of them are very near-term. For example, some AI software could be adopted and implemented much faster than anyone expects, particularly by those who have already shifted their IT budget away from legacy systems and toward both AI development and AI implementation and deployment. In addition to the rapid adoption/implementation risks, there is a legitimate concern regarding gross margin pressure in 2026, when many of the large users will absorb the cost of increased inference costs in the test phase with their customers.

Also, there is a major question about the business model: if AI agents do more and more of the work in front office applications, the current seat based models may be under pressure, especially among software vendors who rely heavily on per user licensing.

Add to all of this the classic macro risks: an economic slowdown, high interest rates, continued inflation, and reduced selectivity in it spending.

As such, I completely understand why investors continue to take a very cautionary approach. Nevertheless, I firmly believe that these represent transition risks and not structural obsolescence risks (at least for companies which control the orchestration layer, possess proprietary data, and/or are well embedded within their customer workflows). In fact, the way that Microsoft generates revenue is fundamentally changing (from per-seat licensing to pay-per-use pricing to enterprise license agreements) and these types of arrangements tend to be tied more directly to the actual value created. Ironically enough, this type of shift in revenue generation has the potential to expand the size of the market rather than limit it. When AI enhances user productivity, ARPU can increase even though the total number of seats does not. Ultimately, growth will depend less upon generating additional license sales and more so upon better monetizing each individual user and each workflow.

Operational Conclusions

If you are going to draw a practical conclusion from what is said here, it should now be clear that the selloff in software stocks in 2026 does not mean the industry as a whole has lost its purpose. In fact, the current market environment can be described as one where nearly all stocks are being sold with very little distinction made on whether they are companies that will ultimately lose their intermediation value via AI or if they will see their position strengthened using AI. That's why I believe there is an enormous opportunity available (perhaps among the largest of the year), particularly in the "work orchestration platforms", in the beneficiaries of infrastructure, and in the vertical software platforms that are demonstrating true monetization.

I also know that I'm taking a lot of risks in stating what I've said in this analysis. I know it goes against the grain, but I'm certain that in the medium to long term, the market will move in this direction.

I would position the investment opportunities within three distinct categories.

  1. The first category is composed of work orchestration platform leaders. Within this category, I would include Microsoft, ServiceNow, and Salesforce. These companies are the ones most likely to continue to drive a company’s decisions because they own the workflow, permissioning, data and coordination across multiple system integrations.
  2. The second category includes the infrastructure beneficiaries, including Snowflake, Datadog, and MongoDB. If AI drives increased compute intensity, data complexity and observability requirements, then I expect each of these platforms to receive fair benefit.
  3. The third category consists of the AI monetization leaders. From this perspective, I would identify Workday, Adobe and Palantir. Here we are no longer discussing narrative – we are discussing AI products generating revenue, ARR, ACV and positive estimate revisions.

Within this category, I consider Workday to have one of the best risk/reward profiles currently available. At its current valuation, Workday still appears to trade as if investors do not completely understand or appreciate the quality of Workday’s execution relative to building a solid AI-based solution. On the other hand, ServiceNow may represent one of the clearest paths toward investing in enterprise orchestration with a well-defined path for future growth related to monetizing AI functionality. Similarly, Snowflake represents one of the most straightforward investments in AI-related infrastructure on both the data/workload side. Adobe represents what I consider to be the most contrarian opportunity of all. Specifically, while the majority of investor focus regarding Adobe has been centered around risk factors and concerns regarding Adobe’s future success and capacity to steer evolving technology trends, I believe that Firefly and related developments in AI monetization represent a major change in how Adobe’s solutions are perceived by customers. Finally, Palantir is arguably the most volatile member of this group however based upon the rate at which estimates for the company have improved over recent months suggests that the underlying business performance is significantly stronger than reflected by the current valuation.

Timing

In terms of timing I believe we need to view the timeline of these events on a longer basis (12–18 months) rather than within a 2-week window. Ultimately, the most significant catalysts for the market will come from second-quarter through third-quarter 2026 earnings reports, at which time the market will have to determine whether the acceleration of contributions to revenue by AI will continue, whether product scaling begins to help create stability to profit margins, and whether the pricing model based upon consumption will be able to offset or possibly exceed, the weakness created in pricing models that are tied to seat counts. This is exactly where the game is being played — If the market believes AI does not reduce the value of the "best" software, but actually creates greater utility and monetization opportunities for this software, then this period will be looked back upon as not the start of the demise of software, but as the beginning of the next growth cycle.

Bottom-line

Ultimately, my thesis is relatively straightforward. AI will not destroy software; however, AI will destroy weak software. Weak software may be defined as software that has been replicated too easily, is too superficially developed, or is poorly integrated into existing systems. However, it will also strengthen those companies that are developing and controlling their own proprietary data and who currently have a role in creating strategic corporate process. When you allow yourself to purchase high-quality technology at today's prices, in my opinion, this represents a market anomaly, not a flaw.

data.title: 3 Best Stocks For A Squeezed Consumer Amid Higher-For-Longer Gas Prices
data.description:
Best Consumer Finance Stocks Amid High Gas Prices

High gas prices and persistent inflation are placing pressure on already stretched U.S. consumer budgets, especially among lower and middle-income households. Gas prices have surged from $2.98 per gallon prior to the Iran war to as high as $4.12 just a week ago, significantly increasing everyday transportation costs. Retail sales figures released by the U.S. Census Bureau on Tuesday revealed gasoline station sales jumped 18% higher than a year ago. While oil prices have fallen roughly $30 from their recent peak, gas prices have only inched down to $4.02, indicating a higher-for-longer budget squeeze for consumers. This can lead to a challenging financial dilemma for many U.S. households as decisions are made to balance filling up the gas tank with paying for essentials like utilities, groceries, and rent. According to a Bloomberg article, pawnshop loans have increased as Americans cash in valuables to pay for gas and necessities.

"We’re making a lot more loans," said Tim Cassidy, the fourth generation in his family to run Cassidy’s Jewelry & Loan in Stockton, Calif. "They have to have that gas, they have to get to work."

Gas Prices Likely to Remain Elevated Throughout 2026

As a result, alternative sources of cash are seeing increased demand. This uptick in demand for consumer finance companies that serve underserved markets is likely to persist, as oil shocks have historically taken time to unwind. The current oil disruption, described as the “largest supply shock on record,” is unlikely to normalize until the end of 2026, according to a Societe Generale report.

Best Consumer Finance Stocks to Buy Now

To select the five best consumer finance stocks to feature in this article, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and chose the pre-selected Top Financial Stocks and filtered for Quant Strong Buys. I then sorted by Industry and Sector and arrived at the three top-Quant-rated stocks in the Consumer Finance industry.

1. EZCORP, Inc. (EZPW)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.89B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/21/2026): 10 out of 681

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/21/2026): 1 out of 38

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Consumer Finance

A holding in my Alpha Picks portfolio, EZCORP is an American leading provider of pawn loans, offering short-term, collateralized loans to consumers who may lack access to traditional credit. Its business model is uniquely positioned for inflationary environments, where rising costs reduce disposable income and increase demand for quick cash to cover non-discretionary expenses. With gas prices surging and household budgets tightening, more consumers turn to pawn transactions to cover short-term cash needs without taking on long-term debt.

In an environment where pawn shop owners are seeing increased demand, EZPW’s above-average P/E is not as reflective of its valuation as the company’s price relative to its strong sales numbers. This also suggests that the high short interest of over 17% is misdirected, increasing the potential for a short squeeze. For example, the 1.38 forward EV/Sales and its 1.19 forward Price/Sales ratios both offer more than 50% discounts to the sector median from a sales perspective. This suggests that EZPW is undervalued on a revenue basis, despite supportive fundamentals, such as its growth metrics.

EZPW’s strong forward EBIT Growth metric of nearly 43% is more than double the sector median’s 12% forward growth rate. This reflects expectations for rising operating income growth, and in EZCORP’s case, it’s being driven by higher pawn loan balances, rising transaction volumes, and improved retail sales of forfeited goods. While unfortunate for many Americans, this a direct reflection of increased consumer stress, translating to higher demand for other niche lenders focused on consumer financing.

2. Enova International, Inc. (ENVA)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.12B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/21/2026): 17 out of 681

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/21/2026): 2 out of 38

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Consumer Finance

Enova International operates a digital platform that provides loan products and online services to underserved consumers. Founded in 2004, Enova has implemented analytics and machine learning algorithms to serve non-prime consumers as well as small businesses. Enova’s flexibility with pricing risk allows it to remain profitable as credit conditions fluctuate. Like our other featured consumer finance companies, higher fuel, insurance, and utility bills are pushing more borrowers toward Enova’s services, driving growth in loan originations and revenue.

Speaking of growth, ENVA's strong Growth Grade reflects a combination of healthy year-over-year revenue as well as forward growth potential. The company’s forward Revenue Growth of nearly 17% looks to repeat its strong 2025 numbers, keeping its revenue above 2x the sector median. More importantly, its forward EPS growth of nearly 32% is more than 121% above sector peers, signaling a company with accelerating earnings power, not just top-line growth. This leads to Enova’s solid profitability metrics.

ENVA’s trailing Gross Profit Margin of nearly 83% is more than 36% better than the sector median, while its 4.77% Return on Assets is well over triple that of its sector peers. The company’s impressive ROA reflects strong pricing and disciplined underwriting, effectively turning its loan portfolio into profits. Most impressive, Enova’s 1.82B trailing Cash From Operations indicates effective loan servicing and supports reinvestment without relying heavily on external funding. ENVA’s financial position reflects adaptability, which positions the company well in an uncertain macro backdrop, particularly given its pending acquisition of Grasshopper Bank for $369M, expected to close in the second half of 2026. As a subsidiary of Enova, Grasshopper’s fintech focus and banking-as-a-service (BaaS) will expand its reach. Grasshopper CEO Mike Butler said:

We’re thrilled to join forces with Enova, a market leader in digital lending and a true innovator in the use of technology and analytics in the financial services sector… This combination of enhanced digital lending and banking will enable us to serve an even broader set of customers while expanding and strengthening the product offerings for our current clients.

This leads to another niche lender focused on consumer financing.

3. Atlanticus Holdings Corporation (ATLC)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.09B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/21/2026): 57 out of 682

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/21/2026): 3 out of 38

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Consumer Finance

Atlanticus Holdings provides credit products through bank partnerships, focusing on underserved consumers through a range of products, including credit cards, point of sale retail credit, and patient healthcare financing. ATLC’s model aims for attractive risk-adjusted returns by targeting customers with limited access to mainstream credit. In an environment where inflation and rising gas prices demand a larger share of consumer purchasing power, Atlanticus stands to benefit from increased consumer reliance on its revolving credit products.

Starting with an 'A' Valuation Grade, ATLC’s forward Non-GAAP P/E of 8.17 reveals a 23.52% discount to the sector median. This is especially remarkable considering the stock is up more than 35% over the past month, managing strong gains through significant market volatility in that time period. An attractive valuation, combined with strong forward growth potential, suggests the stock may continue that momentum in 2026.

Key metrics supporting ATLC's 'A-' Growth Grade begin with its impressive forward Revenue Growth of 38.41%, which places it 358.62% ahead of the sector median. Filtering down through the top-line growth, the company’s forward EPS Growth of over 38% is well over double sector peers, and its sector-crushing Operating Cash Flow underscores its fundamental strength. The only unfortunate side of this financial strength is that it reflects the financial weakness that appears to be deepening for lower- and middle-income households in the U.S. As economic conditions evolve, the broader theme of strained consumer liquidity continues to support the value of specialized lending platforms in 2026.

Conclusion: Inflation Squeeze Drives Surge in Alternative Lending

Higher gas prices and sticky inflation are squeezing consumer budgets, particularly among underserved Americans in lower and middle-income households. This environment is driving increasing reliance on pawn loans, digital lending platforms, and credit cards. Companies in these consumer finance segments are benefiting from rising demand for short-term cash infusions and flexible credit solutions. Looking ahead, if inflation moderates as expected later in 2026, these firms could see a supportive macro tailwind as lower funding costs widen their lending spreads even as demand for their products and services remains strong. Together, this illustrates a challenging environment for U.S. consumers but a compelling setup for EZPW, ENVA, and ATLC.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Subscribe to Premium for unlimited access to Seeking Alpha quant ratings and expert content. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Check out our new Alpha Picks app in the Apple App Store! Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!

data.title: Xero: Focus On AI Partnership And Industry Survey (Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm revising Xero's (XROLF) (XRO.AX) rating from "Hold" to "Buy."

My take is that AI-related disruption risks for XROLF are overstated. Its alliance with Anthropic (ANTHRO) and the read-throughs from a UBS Group (UBS) survey have given me reasons to be optimistic.

My prior April 6, 2025, piece outlined my mixed opinion of the company's forthcoming full-year financial release and its venture into the United States.

Xero-Anthropic Tie-Up Is A Favorable Development

Last month, the firm issued an announcement disclosing "a multi-year deal" between its "global small business platform, and Anthropic, the company behind Claude."

Details Of The Claude-Xero Integration

I've identified multiple positives pertaining to the two entities' co-operation.

At its earlier Feb 3 analyst call, the group noted that "new (artificial intelligence) distribution channels" could drive a "4x TAM expansion for SaaS companies" like itself, citing Gartner's projections. I share this bullishness.

Previously, investors were worried that small business operators would choose AI chatbots over legacy platforms for their accounting needs. I'm sure there are many who are put off by the steep learning curve associated with traditional software. In my view, the ANTHRO-Xero collaboration could help XROLF make its solutions more user-friendly.

I see it generating additional revenues by penetrating these under-served segments via Claude.

Also, XROLF's value proposition is likely to be enhanced by the incorporation of "Claude's advanced reasoning" into its own AI agent called "Just Ask Xero/JAX" as highlighted above.

The enterprise indicated at the early-Feb briefing that its existing "AI-enabled chat and self-service tools are already saving an average of 22 hours per month" for customers. I can imagine JAX becoming even more productive by leveraging Claude.ai.

The XROLF-ANTHRO combination offers a potent mix of high-quality domain-specific data and privacy protection as well.

XROLF's shareholder presentation cites client feedback suggesting that its selling points are "accurate data" and "zero mistakes." I don't think AI-native disruptors can match Xero in these critical aspects.

I need to emphasize the importance of keeping your proprietary information secure. Unlike with general-purpose AI tools, corporations are assured that the integrated Claude/Xero offering won't use their inputs for model training.

Separately, my Apr '25 write-up mentioned that "the American accounting software market is five times as large as that for (home market) Australia."

I believe partnering with ANTHRO is a wise move to enlarge XROLF's U.S. presence too. An April 4 SA News article drew attention to Claude's "rapid user acquisition and deepening engagement" in America as evidenced by its "DAU tripling in a single month (March)."

Market Research Yields Encouraging Findings

UBS published a report (not publicly available) titled "IT Spend Survey" on March 23, 2026. The bank had recently conducted a second yearly poll of a few hundred "Small and Medium-Sized Businesses/SMBs across the United States, UK, Canada, and Australia."

The proportion of respondents aiming to procure AI-related solutions from their current software vendors rose from 2025's 48% to 2026's 55%. I think enterprises prefer sticking with suppliers who house their historical payroll data to ensure workflow continuity.

A substantial high-80s percentage of XROLF's users have the intention of continuing their paid plan subscriptions, according to UBS's fieldwork.

The above-mentioned statistics send a clear message that AI displacement doesn't pose a serious threat to Xero and its peers yet.

UBS's questionnaire results also suggest that companies can accept a price premium of up to 8.5% for incremental AI features.

XROLF might even have the opportunity to charge more for its products following the ANTHRO link-up. That's consistent with the Feb sell-side meeting commentary that its future "subscription gross profit" growth can be realized with either a "price rise for the added value" or "more add-ons."

Current Valuations Aren't Reflective Of Financial Outlook

A simple rule of thumb is that a stock's earnings multiple is roughly equal to its anticipated growth rate. XROLF is attractively priced based on this measure.

The consensus FY25-28 EBITDA CAGR forecast is +20% as per S&P Capital IQ. That's fairly conservative from my perspective. AI can widen its addressable market and provide pricing upside as I've detailed above. Its historical FY2022-2025 annualized increase for the same metric was also a more significant +44%.

Xero's forward one fiscal year EV/EBITDA is 16x. This implies a re-rating potential of +25% (20/16) benchmarked against its future EBITDA expansion.

You Should Weigh These Downside Factors

The XROLF-ANTHRO partnership might not work as well as hoped for. Both companies' management teams can have differing views of how to exploit synergies from this deal. Anthropic may eventually decide it's better to find other like-minded partners or develop their own competing solutions.

The SMBs' opinions regarding AI could change going forward. They might become more price-sensitive if the AI-related add-ons don't deliver the desired results.

My Closing Remarks

I've turned bullish on XROLF.

AI can become more of an opportunity rather than a risk for the firm. It's also undervalued relative to its growth prospects.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brookfield: Finding The Right Mix Of Capital Growth, Hard Assets, And High Margins
data.description:
The Brookfield opportunity comes in many forms, but at the end of the day it is all about efficient capital deployment to achieve desired results. Viewed through that lens, one's decision to invest in one or another part of the Brookfield franchise is better informed with a sound understanding of the underlying entities and operations, and how that impacts one's relationship to this closely-knit group of businesses.

To be precise, choosing the right Brookfield vehicle means knowing what one is investing in so one can decide the manner in which one receives any returns on the investment. It also allows one to control the level of risk one perceives. With that context front and center, I shall try to demonstrate how various Brookfield opportunities can play very dynamic roles alongside a core investment in the parent entity.

Why Brookfield Corporation (BN) as the Core Holding?

Assuming one has decided what a full position in Brookfield looks like, the core holding should be Brookfield Corporation (BN), which anchors one's investments in two other critical subsidiaries, each with its own appeal: Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), and LPs such as Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP), with Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) and Brookfield Infrastructure Corporation (BIPC) being the less tax-tedious equivalents.

BN forms the center of the group's capital flows, holding about $180 billion as its capital base as of Q4 2025. The consolidated books do not give investors the kind of visibility most seek, because it does not offer a true picture of performance and profitability. The 'noise' from each business unit tends to jumble the final picture, so the best way to look at this, in my view, is as a capital growth vehicle that represents the Mother Ship.

This is essentially what one wants to anchor their investment to, which is why I deem it to be a core holding within the Brookfield component of one's total portfolio of investments. This is the whole kit and caboodle, and one should prioritize holding BN as the nucleic center.

That being said, one might not want to settle for capital appreciation, even at a price return CAGR of 15 percent. On a total return basis (including reinvesting the marginal yield), there is no doubt BN is the front-runner of the group, but what if one wants to own the hard assets of the operating businesses or the high margins that come with asset management?

One of the reasons BN should only be a core holding and not the entirety of one's Brookfield investments is that a lot of the underlying liabilities and assets are only meaningful at the subsidiary level. For example, asset-backed debt on BEP's books stays on its books because it is non-recourse. This creates a unique protective shield for BN's own assets - another reason to own the parent first.

Regardless, the return on this investment is limited to capital appreciation and a nominal dividend of 0.60 percent (current forward yield), but the dividend is not the purpose of investing in BN, and this is precisely why it should be layered with two diverse skins, each performing a specific role in one's Brookfield portfolio - an asset play and a high margin play, both offering comparably higher payouts than BN but vastly different risk profiles.

The Hard Assets Play

Both BIP/BIPC and the BEP/BEPC options offer this first additional layer that unlocks income and is inflation-friendly. What I mean by the latter is that these subsidiaries/units are invested in the backbone of the economy - one in hard infrastructure assets and the other in hard energy and alternative energy assets. These investment areas enjoy advantages such as inflation-linked pricing, tax credits, etc.

Under normal circumstances, these LP and Corp units might represent much greater risk because they are more capital-intensive and subject to interest rate pressure and other macro forces beyond the control of these units. Although BN is largely protected from losses and impairments reported by its subsidiaries, it does act like a safety net in times of extreme distress because it is diversified and has ready capital to inject. BN's reported DE or distributable earnings are untouched by non-cash impairments, which is precisely why it acts as the anchor.

Adding this layer of income amplifies the muted distribution yield of the parent.

Where one wants to earn this yield from would depend on whether one is sold on renewable energy or if one prefers to stick with conventional infrastructure investments. I do not see BIP/BIPC and BEP/BEPC as redundant to each other. Each is invested either in inflation-sheltered hard assets or forward-looking energy assets that can take advantage of tax credits, incentives, and other modalities.

There is also an interesting shift underway. While traditional infrastructure and energy projects often involved government backing in the form of FITs or Feed-In-Tariffs and such, newer funding is coming from hyperscalers and large corporations that are actively investing in hard AI assets of their own. These assets need to be powered sustainably, and this is precisely where a vehicle such as BEP/BEPC stands to benefit in the long run. Two examples of this from the BN Annual Report for 2025:

With Google, we signed an agreement to sell them hydro power from two of our facilities in Pennsylvania. These 20-year contracts for over $3 billion of power are among the largest private contracts for hydro power ever signed and will provide baseload power to Google in an important data center market in the U.S.; this in turn will facilitate build-out of their data center operations.

With Microsoft, we continue to build them solar and wind facilities under a framework agreement for over 10.5 gigawatts of power. This is the largest power framework agreement ever signed and will facilitate their growth in renewables to fuel their power needs.

BIP benefits directly from the acceleration in AI infrastructure buildouts, and is already partnering with the biggest of them all - Nvidia Corp (NVDA):

With NVIDIA, we signed a deal to build ai factories on a global basis, and they partnered with us to launch our new fund that will invest in ai infrastructure, including ai factories. This partnership is planned for $100 billion and is one of the first of its kind on a global basis.

In effect, what one accomplishes with this strategy of owning the core investment and the first layer of income-generating assets is to have an anchored position in the strongest of these equities, but supplement the lack of a strong distribution with high-yielding assets owned by the operating entities.

These businesses are precisely where the assets are turned into cash flows, so one should see strong cash conversion cycles. One does, indeed: -35.8 for BEP and -9.6 for BIP, both for Q4 2025.

When choosing between BEP/BEPC and BIP/BIPC, one should consider their appetite for risk and act accordingly. BEP is largely focused on a market that is replacing conventional energy, while BIP is more traditionally aligned to backbone infrastructure but gradually benefiting from these new corporate partnerships in a highly non-conventional space - AI datacenters.

There are other considerations, such as the more complex K-1s issued by LPs. It is one of the reasons BEPC has been so popular, but that additional demand means BEPC typically trades at a higher price and lower yield.

One can effectively assume that BEP prices in the trouble of handling K-1s (hence the lower price momentum), but in exchange provides a better yield. This is the prime consideration for the first layer cladding the BN holding, and one sees a similar pricing pattern with BIP and BIPC.

The choice between the two are essentially guided by your taxation preference because the underlying assets are the same.

The High Margin Play

So, with the core and the hard asset layer, one gets a firm base of capital that is deployed efficiently, and a set of assets that throw out strong cash flows to support a much higher dividend. The capital-intensive nature of the BIP/BIPC-BEP/BEPC play requires an offset with higher margins, and this is represented by BAM.

FRE or fee-related earnings are the focal point for BAM. As of Q4 2025, the unit reported $1.2 trillion worth of assets under management, of which fee-bearing capital was 50 percent, or $603 billion. FRE hit a record of $867 million for a 28 percent growth rate, which bumped DE up 18 percent to $767 million and put BAM in a position to increase the 2026 dividend by 15 percent.

Fee-Bearing Capital or FBC is quite possibly the most important metric in the context of our high margin play thesis. The bulk of these fees are "Long-Term, Permanent or Perpetual", and as of Q4 2025, about 87 percent of total FBC fell into this bucket. This is vital because one does not want a high but unreliable margin, and this high percentage brings in a great deal of FRE visibility.

BAM's high margins, therefore, translate to a strong dividend growth profile.

The Portfolio Strategy

Within the Brookfield slice of one's overall portfolio pie, I would recommend holding BN, BAM, and BIP/BEP or BIPC/BEPC in the following weightings, based on one's core investment objective for this group:

Investment Focus (Mix) BN Weighting (%) BAM Weighting (%) BIP/BEP Weighting (%)
Total Return 60 20 10/10
Income 10 20 35/35
Balanced 50 20 15/15

The TR mix maximizes capital gains but retains significant weightage to assets and high margins, while the Income mix does the opposite - prioritize yield but retain the BN anchor, and the Balanced mix benefits from a more measured risk management approach. None of these are right or wrong, so consider tweaking these weightings to suit your specific investment goals.

In the end, what one is after is the right risk profile for the desired return profile, and the Brookfield family offers all possible combinations for long-term investors of nearly any type. I rate these vehicles a clear long-term Buy and hold.
